issues.original: The "difficulty" of Linux rarely lives in the commands themselves. The hard part is whether you have a clear *map* of the system: why it dominates servers, what multi-user and per-file permissions actually buy you, what changes when you switch between Debian and Red Hat lineages, and what to do in the first ten minutes after an SSH prompt opens.
issues.fixed: The difficulty of Linux rarely lies in the commands themselves. The hard part is having a clear *map* of the system: why it dominates servers, what multi-user and per-file permissions offer, what changes when switching between Debian and Red Hat lineages, and what to do in the first ten minutes after an SSH prompt opens.

issues.original: This post is the **entry guide** for the entire Linux series. It first builds the mental model — philosophy, distributions, the FHS tree — and then walks you through the commands you will use ten times an hour: [CODE], [CODE], [CODE], [CODE], plus pipelines, redirection, SSH, and a quick taste of permissions and processes.
issues.fixed: This post serves as the **entry guide** for the entire Linux series. It first builds the mental model — philosophy, distributions, the FHS tree — and then walks you through the commands you'll use ten times an hour: [CODE], [CODE], [CODE], [CODE], plus pipelines, redirection, SSH, and a quick taste of permissions and processes.

issues.original: Each topic is intentionally **kept short**; depth lives in the dedicated articles (File Permissions, Disk Management, User Management, Service Management, Process Management, Package Management, Advanced File Operations).
issues.fixed: Each topic is intentionally **kept short**; deeper details are in the dedicated articles (File Permissions, Disk Management, User Management, Service Management, Process Management, Package Management, Advanced File Operations).

issues.original: Three design decisions explain almost every Linux quirk a newcomer notices: it was built for **many users at once**, it treats **files as the universal interface**, and it expects **automation over clicking**.
issues.fixed: Three design decisions explain most Linux quirks a newcomer notices: it was built for **multiple users at once**, it treats **files as the universal interface**, and it prioritizes **automation over clicking**.

issues.original: A 5 MB Alpine container and a 12 GB Oracle Linux install both call themselves Linux.
issues.fixed: Both a 5 MB Alpine container and a 12 GB Oracle Linux install call themselves Linux.

issues.original: Production servers routinely run for years without a reboot. The [CODE] command on a long-lived machine printing [CODE] is a normal sight, not a brag.
issues.fixed: Production servers often run for years without a reboot. The [CODE] command on a long-lived machine printing [CODE] is a common sight, not a brag.

issues.fixed: You rarely download a [CODE]. [CODE], [CODE], [CODE], [CODE] resolve dependencies, verify signatures, and allow you to upgrade the entire system with one command.

issues.original: Graphical desktops exist (GNOME, KDE), but on servers you connect over SSH and drive the box with text — which is also what makes scripting and remote management trivial.
issues.fixed: Graphical desktops like GNOME and KDE exist, but on servers, you connect via SSH and manage the system with text, which also makes scripting and remote management easy.

issues.original: Most distros descend from a small number of ancestors. The package manager is usually the fastest way to tell them apart.
issues.fixed: Most distributions descend from a few ancestors. The package manager is usually the quickest way to tell them apart.

issues.original: Pick **Ubuntu LTS** if you have no other constraint.
issues.fixed: Pick **Ubuntu LTS** if you have no other constraints.

issues.original: After CentOS Linux was discontinued in 2021, **Rocky Linux** and **AlmaLinux** became the binary-compatible drop-in replacements.

issues.original: Rolling release, latest packages, expects you to read the wiki.
issues.fixed: Rolling release, latest packages, and expects you to read the wiki.

issues.original: For cloud, also check what your provider blesses: AWS ships **Amazon Linux**, Alibaba Cloud ships **Alibaba Cloud Linux**, both are RHEL derivatives tuned for the platform.
issues.fixed: For cloud, also check what your provider supports: AWS ships **Amazon Linux**, and Alibaba Cloud ships **Alibaba Cloud Linux**. Both are RHEL derivatives tuned for the platform.

issues.original: Dozens of users may be logged in at once over SSH or local TTYs, each running many processes in parallel. The kernel must isolate their CPU time, memory, files, and network sockets. This is the reason the permission model is strict: without it, any user could read another user's secrets or kill another user's processes.
issues.fixed: Dozens of users may log in simultaneously over SSH or local TTYs, each running many processes in parallel. The kernel must isolate their CPU time, memory, files, and network sockets. This is why the permission model is strict: without it, any user could read another user's secrets or kill their processes.

Memorising magic numbers does not get you out of any of these. What does is understanding three things at the same time:

Memorizing magic numbers won't solve these issues. Understanding three key concepts will:

issues.original: The **same [CODE]/[CODE]/[CODE] bits mean different things on a regular file than on a directory** — the directory case is what trips most people up.
issues.fixed: The **same [CODE]/[CODE]/[CODE] bits have different meanings for regular files and directories** — the directory case often confuses people.

issues.original: Linux is a multi-user system, so every inode (file, directory, symlink, device, …) carries three identity hooks:

issues.original: That ordering has a practical consequence that surprises people: **if the owner's bits forbid an action, being in the group does not help**. [CODE] makes the owner unable to read their own file even though "others" can read/write/execute it.
issues.fixed: This ordering has a surprising practical consequence: **if the owner's bits forbid an action, being in the group doesn't help**. [CODE] can make the owner unable to read their own file, even if "others" can read, write, and execute it.

Each bit is one of:

- [CODE] (1) — execute (or, on a directory, "traverse")

issues.original: Most people learn package management as three commands: [CODE], [CODE], [CODE]. That works until something goes wrong - a dependency conflict, an upgrade that won't apply, a kernel that doesn't boot, a mirror that times out from inside China. At that point you need a model of what is actually happening: what a *package* contains, what the *manager* is solving for, where it stores state, and how the difference between Debian's [CODE] and Red Hat's [CODE] shows up at 2 a.m. on a production box.
issues.fixed: Most people learn package management with three commands: [CODE], [CODE], [CODE]. This works until something goes wrong—a dependency conflict, an upgrade that fails, a kernel that won’t boot, or a mirror that times out in China. At that point, you need to understand what’s actually happening: what a *package* contains, what the *manager* is solving for, where it stores state, and how the differences between Debian's [CODE] and Red Hat's [CODE] show up at 2 a.m. on a production box.

issues.original: This article is the model plus the cookbook. We start with what is in a [CODE] or [CODE] file and why a manager is needed at all. Then we walk through [CODE], [CODE] and [CODE] side by side - not just 'the equivalent commands' but where they share assumptions and where they diverge (dependency resolution, version pinning, downgrade, repo trust). After that we cover the things you only learn the hard way: configuring a domestic mirror that actually works, building Nginx from source when the repo version is too old, dropping in a binary tarball like the JDK without [CODE], and a small set of habits that keep production boxes upgradable for years.
issues.fixed: This article provides both the model and the cookbook. We start by explaining what’s in a [CODE] or [CODE] file and why a manager is needed. Then we compare [CODE], [CODE], and [CODE]—not just their equivalent commands, but also where they share assumptions and where they differ (dependency resolution, version pinning, downgrade, repo trust). After that, we cover the things you only learn the hard way: configuring a domestic mirror that works, building Nginx from source when the repo version is too old, adding a binary tarball like the JDK without [CODE], and a few habits that keep production boxes upgradable for years.

issues.original: A package is a single file ([CODE], [CODE], [CODE]) that contains everything one piece of software needs *plus* metadata describing how it should be installed. The metadata is what makes a package a package - without it you have a tarball.
issues.fixed: A package is a single file ([CODE], [CODE], [CODE]) that contains everything a piece of software needs, plus metadata describing how it should be installed. The metadata is what makes a package a package; without it, you have a tarball.

issues.original: On top of those payloads, the package metadata records: the package name and version, dependencies ([CODE] in [CODE], [CODE] in [CODE]), conflicts, post-install scripts (e.g. [CODE]), and a SHA256 of every file. That last bit is what lets [CODE] or [CODE] later tell you which files have been tampered with.
issues.fixed: In addition to these payloads, the package metadata includes the package name and version, dependencies ([CODE] in [CODE], [CODE] in [CODE]), conflicts, post-install scripts (e.g., [CODE]), and a SHA256 hash of every file. This last part allows [CODE] or [CODE] to later identify which files have been tampered with.

issues.original: Without one you would, for every piece of software: 1. Find a download link (and trust whoever runs the site). 2. Manually resolve dependencies - A needs B 1.1+, B needs C, C conflicts with D you already installed. 3. Decide where each file goes. 4. Remember every file you copied so you can uninstall it later. 5. Poll upstream by hand for security updates.
issues.fixed: Without a package manager, for every piece of software, you would: 1. Find a download link (and trust the site). 2. Manually resolve dependencies—A needs B 1.1+, B needs C, C conflicts with D, which you already installed. 3. Decide where each file goes. 4. Remember every file you copied so you can uninstall it later. 5. Check for security updates manually.

issues.original: The Linux ecosystem split into a handful of package families decades ago and has been remarkably stable since. The names you actually need to know:
issues.fixed: The Linux ecosystem split into a few package families decades ago and has remained remarkably stable since. Here are the names you need to know:

issues.original: A "service" on Linux is a long-running background process whose job is to be there when something needs it: synchronise the clock, listen for SSH connections, accept HTTP requests, run a backup at 3 AM. You almost never start one of these by hand. Something has to start them at boot, restart them when they crash, capture their logs, decide what depends on what, and shut everything down cleanly when the machine powers off. On every modern distribution that something is **systemd**.
issues.fixed: A "service" on Linux is a long-running background process that's ready when needed: synchronize the clock, listen for SSH connections, accept HTTP requests, or run a backup at 3 AM. You rarely start these manually. They need to be started at boot, restarted if they crash, have their logs captured, and dependencies managed. They also need to be shut down cleanly when the machine powers off. On modern distributions, **systemd** handles this.

issues.original: This article is the working model I wish someone had handed me when I first got dropped into a production server. We start from why a dedicated service manager exists at all, build up the unit/target mental model, walk through the [CODE] commands you actually use day to day, dissect a unit file line by line, and then cover the adjacent surfaces: [CODE] for logs, [CODE] units as the modern cron, and a step-by-step playbook for "the service won't start."
issues.fixed: This article is the working model I wish I had when I first worked on a production server. We start by explaining why a dedicated service manager is necessary, build the unit/target mental model, walk through the [CODE] commands you use daily, dissect a unit file line by line, and cover related topics: [CODE] for logs, [CODE] units as the modern cron, and a step-by-step playbook for when a service won't start.

issues.original: A bare Linux kernel knows how to load drivers, mount the root filesystem, and execute exactly one program (PID 1). Everything else is somebody else's problem. Historically that "somebody" was **SysV init**: a small program that read scripts out of [CODE] and ran them, in alphabetical order, one after another. Each script was a hand-rolled shell program responsible for forking the daemon into the background, writing a PID file, and pretending to know whether the service was actually up.
issues.fixed: A bare Linux kernel can load drivers, mount the root filesystem, and execute one program (PID 1). Everything else is someone else's problem. Historically, that someone was **SysV init**: a small program that read scripts from [CODE] and ran them in alphabetical order. Each script was a custom shell program that forked the daemon into the background, wrote a PID file, and tried to determine if the service was running.

issues.original: That approach worked, but it accumulated three structural problems: 1. **Serial startup is slow.** Running 80 init scripts sequentially on a modern server wastes most of a multi-core CPU just waiting on I/O. 2. **Dependencies live in the scripts themselves.** If [CODE] needs the network up first, you encode that by hoping the alphabet cooperates ([CODE] before [CODE]) and by sprinkling [CODE] calls through the scripts. It is exactly as fragile as it sounds. 3. **There is no shared concept of state.** Each daemon decides for itself how to background, where to write its PID, how to log, how to restart on crash. The init system has no way to ask "is sshd actually running?" beyond "did this script exit 0?"
issues.fixed: That approach worked, but it had three structural problems: 1. **Serial startup is slow.** Running 80 init scripts sequentially on a modern server wastes most of a multi-core CPU waiting on I/O. 2. **Dependencies are in the scripts.** If [CODE] needs the network, you hope the alphabet works ([CODE] before [CODE]) and sprinkle [CODE] calls through the scripts. This is very fragile. 3. **No shared state.** Each daemon decides how to background, where to write its PID, how to log, and how to restart. The init system can only check if a script exited with 0.

issues.original: systemd replaces all of that with a uniform supervisor that knows the difference between "the start command returned" and "the service is ready," parallelises whatever it can, and keeps a structured log of everything that happens.
issues.fixed: systemd replaces all of that with a uniform supervisor that distinguishes between "the start command returned" and "the service is ready," parallelizes tasks, and keeps a structured log of everything that happens.

issues.original: If you only ever ran [CODE] and [CODE] on a single laptop, you can probably get away without thinking about any of this. The moment more than one human (or more than one service) shares a host, "user management" stops being paperwork and starts being the security model: it decides who can log in, which UID owns the files a process writes, which commands [CODE] will lift to root, and how long a stolen password remains useful.
issues.fixed: If you only ever run [CODE] and [CODE] on a single laptop, you can probably ignore all this. When more than one person or service shares a host, user management becomes a security model. It determines who can log in, which UID owns the files a process writes, which commands [CODE] elevates to root, and how long a stolen password remains useful.
